text:
Eighteen months ago, every pricing deck I reviewed had the same slide: AI as a premium tier, 30-50% uplift, gross margin expansion baked in.

That slide is aging badly.

Buyers stopped paying the AI tax. They tried the add-on, didn't see proportional value, and either churned the upgrade or used it as leverage at renewal. Vendors are folding AI back into core plans and calling it a feature, not a SKU.

The pattern I keep seeing in pricing decisions right now looks like this:

Start with the premium tier. Watch attach rates stall. Switch to credits or usage to make value visible. Realize credits create buyer anxiety and finance friction. Re-bundle into core, raise the base price a bit, compete on outcomes.

Most teams are somewhere in step 2 or 3 and don't know step 5 is coming.

The quieter shift underneath all of this: AI is becoming table stakes faster than any feature category I've tracked. If your 2026 revenue plan assumes a standalone AI tier carries the number, that plan has a hole in it.

Is AI still a SKU in your roadmap, or have you started treating it as the price of staying in the category?

#AIProduct #SaaS #ProductManagement #B2BSaaS #AIStrategy #ProductStrategy
topic: AI features in B2B SaaS products

diagram.title: THE GREAT AI RE-BUNDLING
diagram.subtitle: How SaaS pricing for AI is collapsing back to core plans

steps.label: PREMIUM TIER
steps.description: AI launches as a paid add-on with a 30-100% uplift.

steps.label: ATTACH STALLS
steps.description: Buyers test it, don't see proportional value, downgrade at renewal.

steps.label: CREDITS PIVOT
steps.description: Vendors switch to usage credits to expose value per call.

steps.label: CREDIT FATIGUE
steps.description: Finance teams reject unpredictable bills, buyers cap usage.

steps.label: RE-BUNDLE
steps.description: AI folds into core plans, base price rises, outcomes become the wedge.
